coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots from wasted enzymatic hydrolysis lignin catalyzed by organic acids for UV shielding and antioxidant fluorescent flexible film
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiaoxiao
Liu, Xutong
Su, Yuhang
Jiang, Tianyu
Li, Dongna
Ma, Xiaojun
coredata.dc:description: Biomass wastes have been demonstrated to be sufficient sources for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using green and sustainable hydrothermal technology, and has been applied in many fields. However, the yield of CQDs from biomass directly hydrothermal is extremely low. This study aimed at prove the feasibility of conducting blue emissive CQD by a hydrothermal technology using enzymatic hydrolysis lignin (EHL) from biomass waste as the precursor and three organic acids such as citric acid (CA), DL-malic acid (DLMA) and oxalic acid (OA) for modification. HR-TEM analysis revealed that CQDs modified by organic acids had superior water solubility and smaller particle size than that of CQDs without organic acids (EHL CQDs). In particular, the citric acid-modified CQDs (CA-EHL CQDs) resulted in an increase in the fluorescence intensity and effective quantum yield (QY) up to 500 % and 4.5 %, respectively, displaying a narrower size distribution and a mean particle size of ca. 3.1 nm. In addition, the CQDs/PVA fluorescent nanocomposite films with UV shielding and antioxidant capacity were also developed in this work, which proved great application prospect in information protection, anti-counterfeiting materials, food packaging and other fields. These results are great contributions towards adopting low-cost, sustainable and green process to produce CQDs with high quantum efficiency and expanding the application range of lignin based CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Lignin
Fluorescent films
UV-shielding
Antioxidant capacity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs/PVA fluorescent nanocomposite films
Optical properties of CQDs/PVA fluorescent nanocomposites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31870564 ).

The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Xiaojun ma reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and particle size distribution (in the illustration) of EHL CQDs (a), CA-EHL CQDs (b), DLMA-EHL CQDs (c) and OA-EHL CQDs (d).
XRD (a) FT-IR (b) and XPS (full spectrum (c), C1s spectrum (d) and O1s spectrum (e)) spectra of EHL CQDs, CA-EHL CQDs, DLMA-EHL CQDs and OA-EHL CQDs; the possible structure (f) of CQDs.

UV–vis (a), FL spectra ((b) in illustration: FL spectra of EHL CQDs, CA-CQDs, DLMA-CQDs and OA-CQDs) of EHL CQDs, CA-EHL CQDs, DLMA-EHL CQDs and OA-EHL CQDs; and (c) the optical photos of different CQDs solutions.
FL spectra of EHL CQDs (a), CA-EHL CQDs (b), DLMA-EHL CQDs (c) and OA-EHL CQDs (d) at different excitation wavelengths.
FL spectra of EHL CQDs (a), CA-EHL CQDs (b), DLMA-EHL CQDs (c) and OA-EHL CQDs (d) at different pH.
SEM of residues of EHL CQDs (a), CA-EHL CQDs (b), DLMA-EHL CQDs (c) and OA-EHL CQDs (d).

The digital photos of nanocomposite membrane in daylight (a) and in 365 nm UV light (b); UV-transmittance (c and d) and DPPH clearance curve (e) of CQDs/PVA fluorescent nanocomposites (A: EHL CQDs/PVA, B: CA-EHL CQDs/PVA, C: DLMA-EHL CQDs/PVA, D: OA-EHL CQDs/PVA); (f) the digital photo of "TUST" handwritten on filter paper using CQDs/PVA mixed solution under daylight and UV light (365 nm).
Comparison of previously study about carbon quantum dots derived from bio-wastes precursors.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots from wasted enzymatic hydrolysis lignin catalyzed by organic acids for UV shielding and antioxidant fluorescent flexible film

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Light Industry Science and Engineering, Tianjin University of Science & Technology, Tianjin 300222, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Light Industry Science and Engineering, Tianjin University of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Light Industry Science and Engineering, Tianjin University of Science & Technology, Tianjin 300222, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Fujian Polytechnic Normal University, Fuqing 350300, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Fujian Polytechnic Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Fujian Polytechnic Normal University, Fuqing 350300, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xiaoxiao Li and Xutong Liu have contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass wastes have been demonstrated to be sufficient sources for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using green and sustainable hydrothermal technology, and has been applied in many fields. However, the yield of CQDs from biomass directly hydrothermal is extremely low. This study aimed at prove the feasibility of conducting blue emissive CQD by a hydrothermal technology using enzymatic hydrolysis lignin (EHL) from biomass waste as the precursor and three organic acids such as citric acid (CA), DL-malic acid (DLMA) and oxalic acid (OA) for modification. HR-TEM analysis revealed that CQDs modified by organic acids had superior water solubility and smaller particle size than that of CQDs without organic acids (EHL CQDs). In particular, the citric acid-modified CQDs (CA-EHL CQDs) resulted in an increase in the fluorescence intensity and effective quantum yield (QY) up to 500 % and 4.5 %, respectively, displaying a narrower size distribution and a mean particle size of ca. 3.1 nm. In addition, the CQDs/PVA fluorescent nanocomposite films with UV shielding and antioxidant capacity were also developed in this work, which proved great application prospect in information protection, anti-counterfeiting materials, food packaging and other fields. These results are great contributions towards adopting low-cost, sustainable and green process to produce CQDs with high quantum efficiency and expanding the application range of lignin based CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of carbon materials, have attracted extensive attention at present due to their excellent water solubility, low toxicity, unique photoluminescence properties and functionalization (Kang et al., 2020; P.K et al., 2021; Shaik et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2020b). In 2004, CQDs were first reported when Xu group accidentally observed an about 10 nm quasi-spherical nanoparticle in the preparation of electrophoretic purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (Xu et al., 2004). Since then, various preparation methods of CQDs have been developed successively, including top-down (arc discharge, laser ablation and electrochemical oxidation, etc.) and bottom-up (hydrothermal method, template method and plasma treatment, etc.) two categories of methods (Anwar et al., 2019; Ji et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020b). The "top-down" synthesis involves physically or chemically stripping large carbon matrix precursor (eg. carbon fiber, graphite rod, carbon ash and activated carbon, etc.) into tiny CQDs. "Bottom-up" synthesis is the opposite of "top-down" synthesis, in which CQDs are synthesized from carbon materials of very small sizes, such as organic small molecules or oligomers (citric acid, glucose, urea, amino acids, ionic liquid) as carbon sources. Among them, hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs has been widely concerned because of its advantages of simple preparation, adjustable reaction conditions, low energy consumption and environmental protection. Particularly, the excellent optical performance of CQDs makes it a preferable substitute for bioimaging (Baragau et al., 2021; Bhunia et al., 2013), ion detection (Jing et al., 2019; Kalaiyarasan et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2020), drug delivery (Khorsandi et al., 2021; Molaei, 2019; Sousa et al., 2021), photocatalysis (Qin and Zeng, 2017) and inkjet printing dyes (Bao et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2020; Jin et al., 2021).

Most carbonaceous substances in nature can be used as precursors to prepare CQDs (Perumal et al., 2021). Among them, biomass has superior value and prospects due to its low cost, renewable, abundant and environmentally friendly characteristics (Park et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020a, 2020b; Zulfajri et al., 2019). Recently, many researches have reported that various biomass can be used as carbon precursors to synthesize CQDs, such as vegetables (Alam et al., 2013; Feng et al., 2015), fruits (Atchudan et al., 2016; Mehta et al., 2015), leaves (Liu et al., 2014; Song et al., 2017), and wastes (Hong and Yang, 2021; Hu et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2012). However, compared with small molecular organics such as ethylenediamine, the practical application of CQDs from biomass was limited by weak fluorescence, especially lignocellulosic biomass. Lignin, the second largest terrestrial polymer after cellulose (the only aromatic compound in plant cell wall), is a class of sources widely complex organic polymers found in nature with natural renewable, non-toxic, inexpensive unique characteristics (Chio et al., 2019). Numerous literatures had presented that lignin could be used as the precursor of CQDs to realize its high-value utilization and reduce CQDs cost (Yang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2019). However, it is very difficult to obtain the high quantum yield (QY) of lignin-based CQDs for broadening application scope. Herein, it is crucial to establish a green and sustainable technological route to produce high-yield and available lignin-based CQDs.

In this work, lignin-based CQDs were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal technology. Enzymatic hydrolysis lignin (EHL) from rubber wood was used as carbon precursor, while organic acids (citric acid, DL-malic acid and oxalic acid) was introduced as the catalyzer. The effects of different organic acids on the micro morphology, quantum yield（QY), photoluminescence (PL) and pH response of lignin-based CQDs were analyzed in detail by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. Then, CQDs/PVA fluorescent nanocomposite films were established by using prepared lignin-based CQDs, and the fluorescence, light transmittance, UV shielding and oxidation resistance of CQDs/PVA fluorescent nanocomposite films were further researched.

EHL was the residue of rubber powder enzymatic hydrolysis under the action of cellulase in the laboratory. Sodium hydroxide (AR, 98 %) and hydrochloric acid (AR, 36–38 %) were provided by Sinopharm Reagent Co., Ltd. The citric acid (AR, 99.5 %), DL Malic acid (AR, 99.5 %), and oxalic acid (AR, 99.5 %) were acquired from Suzhou Huateng Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. The dialysis bag (retaining molecular weight: 500 Da) was provided by Nanjing Senbega Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Lab-made distilled water was utilized in whole experiments.

1 g purified EHL and 0.24 g organic acids (citric acid (CA), DL malic acid (DLMA) and oxalic acid (OA)) respectively, were dispersed in 20 mL of distilled water under sonicate for 15 min. The mixture was sealed in a hydrothermal autoclave and reacted at 220 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered by vacuum filtering with a 0.22 µm filter membrane. The obtained yellow filtrate was dialyzed in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 500 for 48 h in 1 L deionized water, and the water was changed every 6 h. Finally, CQDs powders were obtained after freeze-drying for 3 days. The above samples were labeled as EHL CQDs, CA-EHL CQDs, DLMA-EHL CQDs and OA-EHL CQDs respectively. As control sample, the method of prepared CQDs from EHL and organic acid was the same as above conditions, the obtained CQDs were designed as EHL CQDs, CA CQDs, DLMA CQDs and OA CQDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs/PVA fluorescent nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: PVA (7.5 % of mass fraction) was dissolved in the CQDs aqueous solution (0.2 mg/mL), and heated in a water bath at 90 °C for 2 h under magnetic stirring to obtain a CQDs/PVA mixed solution. Then, the mixed solution was defoamed at 90 °C for 30 min. Whereafter, the uniform mixed solution was cast on a glass plate (20 ×20 ×2 cm) and dried at room temperature to form a functional film with the properties of UV-shielding, transparence, and antioxidant capacity. The above samples corresponded to PVA, EHL CQDs/PVA, CA-EHL CQDs/PVA, DLMA-EHL CQDs/PVA and OA-EHL CQDs/PVA, respectively.

The microscopic morphology and particle size distribution of CQDs were obtained via transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-F2100) at 200 kV. 15 μL of CQDs filtrate was dropped on an ultra-thin carbon film copper mesh and dried at room temperature to prepare a TEM measurement sample. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were monitored with a TD-3500 spectrometer (Tongda, Liaoning). The functional group structure of CQDs was characterized at room temperature by FT-IR spectrophotometer (Nicolet iS5, Germany). The FT-IR test samples were acquired by mixing CQDs and KBr powders to prepare compressed tablets. Morphology of residues after hydrothermal reaction were observed through field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, FEI_APREO) at 10 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was analyzed through an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (K-Alpha, MultiLab 2000, Thermo Scientific). The absorption spectra of CQDs and UV-transmittance of CQDs/PVA nanocomposite films were measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2007, Japan). Fluorescence spectra (FL) of CQDs was studied via F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer at a voltage of 500 V and a slit of 5 nm.

The quantum yield (QY) of lignin-based CQDs was calculated with quinine sulfate as a reference. And the specific calculation formula was as follows:
(1)
Q
x
=
Q
re
A
re
A
x
I
x
I
re
η
x
2
η
re
2
Where Q, A, I and η are the quantum efficiency, absorbance at suitable excitation wavelengths, emission spectral intensity and refractive index of aqueous solution respectively. And x is the samples to be tested, re is the reference. Take quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (Q
re = 0.54, η
re = 1.33) as a reference. In order to ensure the reliability of experimental data, maintain the light absorption value at the excitation wavelength < 0.1.

To evaluate the pH sensibility of CQDs, 0.5 mL CQDs solutions (0.2 mg/mL) was added into solutions with different pH values for 5 min. Then observe the fluorescence changes of CQDs in different pH solutions. HCl and NaOH were used to adjust the pH solution.

According to previous method (Shi et al., 2009), DPPH radical scavenging method was used to assessed the antioxidant abilities of CQDs/PVA nanocomposites. 0.5 g composites were cut into pieces and dissolved into 10 mL methanol solution, so that the composites were completely forbidden in methanol solution, and soaked at room temperature for 24 h. Then, 2 mL of supernatant and 2.5 mL DPPH methanol solution (40 mg/L) were mixed evenly and stored under dark conditions for 1 h. Read the absorbance values of different solution at 517 nm. The free radical scavenging rate was calculated as follows:
(2)
( RSA ,
% )
=
( 1 −
A
s
A
c
)
× 100
where Ac is the UV absorbance value of DPPH methanol aqueous solution, and As is the UV absorbance value of composites/DPPH mixture solution. All experiments were repeated 3 times to get the average value.

The TEM results ( Fig. 1a-d) depicted that CQDs were monodisperse nanoparticles with spherical shape. Particularly, the CQDs modified by organic acid had a smaller spherical particle size and a uniform particle distribution. It was observed from HR-TEM (the illustrations in Fig. 1a-d) that CQDs had a uniform and clear lattice spacing (0.21 nm), which corresponded to the (100) lattice planes of graphite. Particle distribution of CQDs was obtained by measuring about 100 carbon particles in TEM images of CQDs using Nano Measurer software (the illustrations in Fig. 1a-d), the size distribution histogram and normal distribution graph emphasized that the average particle size of these four points followed the order of CA-EHL CQDs (3.1 nm) < DLMA-EHL (4.7 nm) < OA-EHL CQDs (5.8 nm) < EHL CQDs (6.2 nm). Importantly, CA-EHL CQDs possessed good water solubility and narrower particle size distribution.

The XRD patterns ( Fig. 2a) displayed that EHL CQDs, CA-EHL CQDs, DLMA-EHL CQDs and OA-EHL CQDs had a broad peak at 2θ = 22.6°, 22.0°, 22.3°, 22.5°, respectively, which corresponded to the (002) peak of graphite carbon, revealing the presence of amorphous carbon structure (Zheng et al., 2010). In addition, the sharper peaks of CQDs modified with organic acids were attributed to their higher hydrothermal carbonization degree. FT-IR was examined to analyze the effect of the structure and functional groups of CQDs on fluorescence emission. All CQDs exhibited similar FT-IR spectra and all retained the basic structure of EHL (Fig. 2b). The presence of characteristic absorption at 3420 cm−1 and 1740 cm−1 were owed to the tensile vibration of -OH and CO bounds, respectively. The existence of a host of oxygen-containing functional groups led to excellent hydrophilic properties of CQDs. The absorption peaks at 2910 cm−1 and 1514 cm−1 were derived from C-H and CC bounds stretching vibration in the aromatic structure of CQDs, respectively. The bounds at 1215 cm−1 and 1114 cm−1 were originated from stretching vibrations of phenolic C-OH and C-O-C of the lignin structure. Comparing with EHL CQDs, the CO peak at 1740 cm−1 of CQDs modified by organic acids was enhanced, while the C-O-C peak at 1121 cm−1 was significantly weakened, which was caused by the rupture and reorganization of C-O-C bond at high temperature.

The elemental composition and surface states of as-prepared CQDs were analyzed by XPS, as depicted in Fig. 2c. The peak positions of CQDs could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 285 eV and 532 eV, corresponding to the C1s and O1s energy levels, respectively. It indicated that the prepared lignin-based CQDs were composed of C and O. The C content of EHL CQDs, CA-EHL CQDs, DLMA-EHL CQDs and OA-EHL CQDs was 73.97 %, 70.92 %, 72.25 % and 72.59 % respectively, and the content of O was 26.03 %, 29.08 %, 27.75 % and 27.41 % respectively. In the high-resolution spectroscopy, C1s speak (Fig. 2d) showed three C-types including SP2 carbon (CC), SP3 carbon (C−O) and carboxyl carbon (CO). O1s speak (Fig. 2e) presented two O-types including (CO) and (C-O). By analyzing XPS data, it was found that the oxygen content of CQDs modified by organic acid significantly increased and the carbon to oxygen ratio decreased, suggesting that the enhancement of the degree of hydrothermal carbonization. Meanwhile, the peak area of CO of CQDs modified by organic acid in O1s spectrum also increased significantly. In particular, the oxygen content and the corresponding peak area of CO of CA-EHL CQDs were the largest, which agreed with the FT-IR and XRD analysis. The possible structure of CQDs could be obtained by analyzing FTIR and XPS data as shown in Fig. 2f.

To study the optical characteristics of CQDs, the UV–vis absorption spectra, FL spectrum and optical photos of CQDs were analyzed in detail in Fig. 3. It could be observed from the Fig. 3a that characteristic absorption peaks appeared at 280 nm for all curves, which were caused by π-π * transition of aromatic structure CC, and the absorption peak at 315 nm was caused by the n-π * transition of CO on CQDs. Combined with FTIR and XPS analysis, the conjugation between functional groups and carbon core on the surface of CQDs had a crucial influence on the fluorescence performance of CQDs.

Compared with EHL CQDs, the FL of CQDs modified by organic acids were improved to different degrees as shown in Fig. 3b. The FL of CA-EHL CQDs, DLMA-EHL CQDs and OA-EHL CQDs were increased to 500 %, 320 % and 180 %, respectively. Moreover, the QY remarkably increased from 0.8 % to 4.5 %, almost 6-fold, and followed the order of CA-EHL CQDs (4.5 %) > DLMA-EHL CQDs (2.4 %) > OA-EHL CQDs (1.4 %) > EHL CQDs (0.8 %). Thus, the addition of organic acids played an important role on improving the FL and QY of EHL CQDs. Table 1 showed the comparison of previously study about carbon quantum dots derived from bio-wastes precursors. The organic acids provided an acidic environment for the mechanism, which promoted the hydrolysis of EHL and the hydrothermal carbonization degree. As a result, the size of CQDs modified by organic acid was smaller, which was consistent with TEM result. In addition, XPS data reflected that CQDs modified by organic acid had the rich carboxylic acid functional groups, which could generate more defect sites on the surface of CQDs, acting as excitation energy traps, and fluorescence recombines the exciton captured by the defect contributing the enhanced FL (Yang et al., 2012).

In addition, organic acids could be used as carbon precursor alone to prepare CQDs. Therefore, CA CQDs, DLMA CQDs and OA CQDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method with CA, DLMA and OA as carbon sources, respectively. The results (inset in Fig. 3b) displayed that the FL of CA-CQDs, DLMA-CQDs and OA-CQDs was lower than that of EHL CQDs and CA-EHL CQDs, DLMA-EHL CQDs and OA-EHL CQDs. The optical photos (Fig. 3c) revealed that different CQDs solutions with the same concentration showed pale yellow transparent solution under daylight, and a bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp. The enhanced fluorescence effect CQDs modified by organic could also be observed obviously. The reason was that CQDs modified with organic acids had a higher hydrothermal carbonization degree. CA-EHL CQDs showed the brightest blue fluorescence particularly. Combined with TEM, FTIR and XPS data, it was noted that the surface of CA-EHL CQDs possessed the highest content of O element, best water solubility, smallest particle size, uniform dispersion and strongest fluorescence. The fluorescence effect of DLMA-EHL CQDs and OA-EHL CQDs was weaker than that of CA-EHL CQDs. More importantly, experimental results verified that organic acids could not only be used as a second carbon source to increase the carbon content in the reaction mechanism, but also provide an acidic environment for hydrothermal reactions to catalyze lignin and increase the production of CQDs.

As shown in Fig. 4, with the change of excitation wavelength from 280 nm to 420 nm, the emission peak positions of CQDs were red-shifted. The emission wavelengths of EHL CQDs, CA-EHL CQDs, DLMA-EHL CQDs and OA-EHL CQDs were red-shifted by 68, 41, 48 and 65 nm, respectively, and the FL showed a trend of first strengthening and then weakening. CQDs showed excitation wavelength dependence, indicating that organic acid modification didn’t change the wavelength dependence of EHL CQDs. However, the emission wavelength range of CQDs modified with organic acids became narrow. The energy distribution and radiation recombination of excited states might be affected by the excitation wavelength. The narrow emission wavelength range of CA-EHL CQDs (from 448 nm to 489 nm), which was consistent with particle size distribution from TEM data because of emission traps on the surface (Park et al., 2014). Moreover, CQDs showed the best fluorescence performance at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm, but the optimal emission wavelengths were 461 nm, 452 nm, 457 nm, and 460 nm, respectively.

In order to investigate the effect of pH on the fluorescence properties of CQDs, 0.5 mL CQDs aqueous solution was put into 4 mL different pH solution, and the FL changes of CQDs solution in different pH solutions were observed to study the effect of pH on the fluorescence properties of CQDs. Fig. 5 revealed that the fluorescence of EHL CQDs and OA-EHL CQDs were greatly affected by external acid-base conditions. When pH > 11, the fluorescence of CQDs decreased significantly and the emission wavelength of that was red-shifted (Fig. 5a-d). Under weak acid and neutral conditions, they had excellent blue fluorescence characteristics. However, CA-EHL CQDs and DLMA-EHL CQDs could still maintain strong fluorescence (Fig. 5b-c) under strong acid and strong base conditions, especially DLMA-EHL CQDs was hardly affected. The above pH change effect might depend on the type and content of functional groups on the surface of the CQDs (Mondal and Shyamal, 2019). The acid-modified lignin-based CQDs possessed plenty of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on their surface. Due to the protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl groups under different pH conditions and the resulting change in electrostatic charging property, the fluorescence emission intensity of the CQDs decreased gradually as the pH value increased. Compared with other CQDs, the change of OA- EHL CQDs was more obvious from pH 11.1–12.1 due to fewer carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface (observed from FT-IR and XPS). As a result, CQDs might be affected by acid-base conditions and could be used as a reference for other researchers.

The remaining residues after hydrothermal reaction were analyzed by SEM to study the influence of organic acid on hydrothermal reaction process, as shown in Fig. 6. After 12 h of hydrothermal reaction, it could be seen that the residues of EHL CQDs showed partial pores and fragments in the lamellar lignin. And the residues of CA-EHL CQDs, DLMA-EHL CQDs and OA-EHL CQDs had more broken structures on the surface of lignin. Obviously, after adding organic acids, the hydrolysis reaction was more complete and the decomposition of lignin was higher. Among them, the lignin in CA-EHL CQDs residue had serious structural damage and more collapse, proving that CA played the best catalytic effect on lignin during hydrolysis. The SEM results of CQDs residues agreed with the above PL data and TEM results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CQDs/PVA fluorescent nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: In order to enrich the function of PVA membrane and further expand its application, CQDs/PVA fluorescence nanocomposite membrane was prepared in this study. It could be seen that prepared composites showed transparent light yellow under daylight, but bright blue fluorescence in 365 nm UV light ( Fig. 7a-b), which were promising in the field of anti-counterfeit packaging such as anti-counterfeiting ink (Fig. 7f). Fig. 7c revealed that CQDs/PVA fluorescent nanocomposites possessed high phototransmittance, especially the phototransmittance of CA-EHL CQDs could reach about 83 %, indicating that the prepared nanocomposite films had excellent transparency. Generally, UV wavelengths caused damage to human skin in the range of 275–400 nm. The UV resistance intensity was mainly measured at UVA (400–320 nm), UVB (320–275 nm) and UVC (275–200 nm) (Huang et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2020). According to Fig. 7d, pure PVA film had no UV shielding performance, and the UV transmittance was up to 90 %, while CQDs/PVA fluorescent nanocomposite films presented excellent UV resistance, almost completely shielding UVC, UVB and most UVA. It could be seen in Fig. 7e that the CQDs/PVA fluorescent nanocomposite has excellent DPPH radical scavenging rates, which were 69.2 %, 90.2 %, 77.7 % and 73.2 %, respectively. It proved the promising application of composite materials in the field of antioxidant packaging.

Based on the above results, a new type of CQDs/PVA fluorescent nanocomposite film had been developed using CQDs modified with organic acids, which endowed the PVA film with anti-ultraviolet and anti-oxidation functions. It was expected to be applied to anti-counterfeiting packaging and food packaging with fresh-keeping and UV-shielding requirements. Therefore, the prepared fluorescent nanocomposite material enriched the functions of PVA and broadened the application range of PVA film.

In summary, we proposed a novel, green, simple and low-cost hydrothermal technology to synthesize blue emissive CQDs through three organic acids (CA, DLMA and OA) from carbon precursor waste EHL. The fluorescence properties and QY of modified CQDs were remarkably improved. Particularly, compared with EHL CQDs, a 500 % meliorative fluorescence intensity of CA-EHL CQDs, and whose effective QY increased from 0.8 % to 4.5 %, almost 6-fold. In addition, the CQDs/PVA fluorescent film prepared in this study entrusted strong UV shielding, antioxidant capacity and application prospects in information protection, anti-counterfeiting materials, food packaging and other fields. This research was a promising method to increase the QY, and exhibited the various application of lignin based CQDs in UV shielding and antioxidant packaging.

Xiaoxiao Li: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data collection. Xutong Liu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Data collection. Yuhang Su: Methodology, Investigation. Tianyu Jiang: Investigation. Dongna Li: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Xiaojun Ma: Overall supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Xiaojun ma reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31870564).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Alam et al., 2013

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of carbon quantum dot from cabbage with down- and up-conversion photoluminescence properties: excellent imaging agent for biomedical application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Alam, A.-M., Park, B.-Y., Ghouri, Z.K., Park, M., Kim, H.-Y., 2013. Synthesis of carbon quantum dot from cabbage with down- and up-conversion photoluminescence properties: excellent imaging agent for biomedical application.
